solution:
First solution: Write $p(n) = n^3 + an^2 + bn + c$. Note that $p(n)$
and $p(n+2)$ have the same parity, and recall that any perfect square
is congruent to 0 or 1 (mod 4). Thus if $p(n)$ and $p(n+2)$ are perfect
squares, they are congruent mod 4. But $p(n+2) - p(n) \equiv 2n^2 + 2b$
(mod 4), which is not divisible by 4 if $n$ and $b$ have opposite parity.
%
% and likewise for $p(n+1)$ and $p(n+3)$.
%But the ``third difference'' of $p$ is $p(n) -
%3p(n+1) + 3p(n+2) - p(n+3) = 6$ (easy calculation), so that $p(n) +
%p(n+1) - p(n+2) - p(n+3) \equiv 2$ (mod 4). Thus not all of $p(n), p(n+1),
%p(n+2), p(n+3)$ can be perfect squares.
%(n+2)^3 + a(n+2)^2 + b(n+2) + c
%n^3 + an^2 + bn + c
%== 2n^2 + 2b

Second solution:
We prove more generally that for any polynomial $P(z)$ with integer
coefficients which is not a perfect square, there exists a positive
integer $n$ such that $P(n)$ is not a perfect square. Of course it
suffices to assume $P(z)$ has no repeated factors, which is to say $P(z)$
and its derivative $P'(z)$ are relatively prime.

In particular, if we carry out the Euclidean algorithm on $P(z)$ and $P'(z)$
without dividing, we get an integer $D$ (the discriminant of $P$) such that
the greatest common divisor of $P(n)$ and $P'(n)$ divides $D$ for any $n$.
Now there exist infinitely many primes $p$ such that $p$ divides $P(n)$ for
some $n$: if there were only finitely many, say, $p_1, \dots, p_k$, then
for any $n$ divisible by $m = P(0) p_1 p_2 \cdots p_k$, we have $P(n)
\equiv P(0) \pmod{m}$, that is, $P(n)/P(0)$ is not divisible by $p_1,
\dots, p_k$, so must be $\pm 1$, but then $P$ takes some value infinitely
many times, contradiction. In particular, we can choose some such $p$ not
dividing $D$, and choose $n$ such that $p$ divides $P(n)$. Then $P(n+kp)
\equiv P(n) + kp P'(n) (\mathrm{mod}\,p)$
(write out the Taylor series of the left side);
in particular, since $p$ does not divide $P'(n)$, we can find some $k$
such that $P(n+kp)$ is divisible by $p$ but not by $p^2$, and so
is not a perfect square.

Third solution: (from David Rusin, David Savitt, and Richard Stanley
independently)
Assume that $n^{3}+an^{2}+bn+c$ is a square for all $n>0$.
For sufficiently large $n$,
\begin{align*}
(n^{3/2} + \frac{1}{2} an^{1/2} - 1)^{2} &< n^{3} + an^{2}+bn+c \\
&<  (n^{3/2}+ \frac{1}{2} an^{1/2}+1)^{2};
\end{align*}
thus if $n$ is a large even perfect square, we have $n^{3}+an^{2}+bn+c =
(n^{3/2} + \frac{1}{2} an^{1/2})^{2}$. We conclude this is an
equality of polynomials, but the right-hand side
is not a perfect square for $n$ an even non-square, contradiction.
(The reader might try generalizing this approach to arbitrary polynomials.
A related argument, due to Greg Kuperberg: write $\sqrt{n^3+an^2+bn+c}$
as $n^{3/2}$ times a power series in $1/n$ and take two finite differences
to get an expression which tends to 0 as $n \to \infty$, contradiction.)

Note: in case $n^3 + an^2 + bn + c$ has no repeated factors, it is a
square for only finitely many $n$, by a theorem of Siegel; work of Baker gives
an explicit (but large) bound on such $n$. (I don't know whether the graders
will accept this as a solution, though.)

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long.solution:
First solution: Write $primevalue(indexer) = indexer^3 + coefalpha indexer^2 + coefbeta indexer + coefgamma$. Note that $primevalue(indexer)$
and $primevalue(indexer+2)$ have the same parity, and recall that any perfect square
is congruent to 0 or 1 (mod 4). Thus if $primevalue(indexer)$ and $primevalue(indexer+2)$ are perfect
squares, they are congruent mod 4. But $primevalue(indexer+2) - primevalue(indexer) \equiv 2 indexer^2 + 2 coefbeta$
(mod 4), which is not divisible by 4 if $indexer$ and $coefbeta$ have opposite parity.
%
% and likewise for $primevalue(indexer+1)$ and $primevalue(indexer+3)$.
%But the ``third difference'' of $primevalue$ is $primevalue(indexer) -
%3primevalue(indexer+1) + 3primevalue(indexer+2) - primevalue(indexer+3) = 6$ (easy calculation), so that $primevalue(indexer) +
%primevalue(indexer+1) - primevalue(indexer+2) - primevalue(indexer+3) \equiv 2$ (mod 4). Thus not all of $primevalue(indexer), primevalue(indexer+1),
%primevalue(indexer+2), primevalue(indexer+3)$ can be perfect squares.
%(indexer+2)^3 + coefalpha(indexer+2)^2 + coefbeta(indexer+2) + coefgamma
%indexer^3 + coefalpha indexer^2 + coefbeta indexer + coefgamma
%== 2 indexer^2 + 2 coefbeta

Second solution:
We prove more generally that for any polynomial $polycapital(zetavariable)$ with integer
coefficients which is not a perfect square, there exists a positive
integer $indexer$ such that $polycapital(indexer)$ is not a perfect square. Of course it
suffices to assume $polycapital(zetavariable)$ has no repeated factors, which is to say $polycapital(zetavariable)$
and its derivative $polycapital'(zetavariable)$ are relatively prime.

In particular, if we carry out the Euclidean algorithm on $polycapital(zetavariable)$ and $polycapital'(zetavariable)$
without dividing, we get an integer $discriminant$ (the discriminant of $polycapital$) such that
the greatest common divisor of $polycapital(indexer)$ and $polycapital'(indexer)$ divides $discriminant$ for any $indexer$.
Now there exist infinitely many primes $primevalue$ such that $primevalue$ divides $polycapital(indexer)$ for
some $indexer$: if there were only finitely many, say, $primevalue_1, \dots, primevalue_{iterater}$, then
for any $indexer$ divisible by $modulus = polycapital(0) primevalue_1 primevalue_2 \cdots primevalue_{iterater}$, we have $polycapital(indexer)
\equiv polycapital(0) \pmod{modulus}$, that is, $polycapital(indexer)/polycapital(0)$ is not divisible by $primevalue_1,
\dots, primevalue_{iterater}$, so must be $\pm 1$, but then $polycapital$ takes some value infinitely
many times, contradiction. In particular, we can choose some such $primevalue$ not
dividing $discriminant$, and choose $indexer$ such that $primevalue$ divides $polycapital(indexer)$. Then $polycapital(indexer+iterater primevalue)
\equiv polycapital(indexer) + iterater primevalue\, polycapital'(indexer) (\mathrm{mod}\,primevalue)$
(write out the Taylor series of the left side);
in particular, since $primevalue$ does not divide $polycapital'(indexer)$, we can find some $iterater$
such that $polycapital(indexer+iterater primevalue)$ is divisible by $primevalue$ but not by $primevalue^2$, and so
is not a perfect square.

Third solution: (from David Rusin, David Savitt, and Richard Stanley
independently)
Assume that $indexer^{3}+coefalpha indexer^{2}+coefbeta indexer+coefgamma$ is a square for all $indexer>0$.
For sufficiently large $indexer$,
\begin{align*}
(indexer^{3/2} + \frac{1}{2} coefalpha indexer^{1/2} - 1)^{2} &< indexer^{3} + coefalpha indexer^{2}+coefbeta indexer+coefgamma \\
&<  (indexer^{3/2}+ \frac{1}{2} coefalpha indexer^{1/2}+1)^{2};
\end{align*}
thus if $indexer$ is a large even perfect square, we have $indexer^{3}+coefalpha indexer^{2}+coefbeta indexer+coefgamma =
(indexer^{3/2} + \frac{1}{2} coefalpha indexer^{1/2})^{2}$. We conclude this is an
equality of polynomials, but the right-hand side
is not a perfect square for $indexer$ an even non-square, contradiction.
(The reader might try generalizing this approach to arbitrary polynomials.
A related argument, due to Greg Kuperberg: write $\sqrt{indexer^3+coefalpha indexer^2+coefbeta indexer+coefgamma}$
as $indexer^{3/2}$ times a power series in $1/indexer$ and take two finite differences
to get an expression which tends to 0 as $indexer \to \infty$, contradiction.)

Note: in case $indexer^3 + coefalpha indexer^2 + coefbeta indexer + coefgamma$ has no repeated factors, it is a
square for only finitely many $indexer$, by a theorem of Siegel; work of Baker gives
an explicit (but large) bound on such $indexer$. (I don't know whether the graders
will accept this as a solution, though.)

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Prove that, for any integers $crocodile, sunflower, lemonade$, there exists a positive integer
$marshmallow$ such that $\sqrt{marshmallow^3+crocodile\,marshmallow^2+sunflower\,marshmallow+lemonade}$ is not an integer.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First solution: Write $toothbrush(marshmallow) = marshmallow^3 + crocodile\,marshmallow^2 + sunflower\,marshmallow + lemonade$. Note that $toothbrush(marshmallow)$
and $toothbrush(marshmallow+2)$ have the same parity, and recall that any perfect square
is congruent to $0$ or $1$ (mod $4$). Thus if $toothbrush(marshmallow)$ and $toothbrush(marshmallow+2)$ are perfect
squares, they are congruent mod $4$. But $toothbrush(marshmallow+2) - toothbrush(marshmallow) \equiv 2\,marshmallow^2 + 2\,sunflower$ (mod $4$), which is not divisible by $4$ if $marshmallow$ and $sunflower$ have opposite parity.
%
% and likewise for $toothbrush(marshmallow+1)$ and $toothbrush(marshmallow+3)$.
%But the ``third difference'' of $toothbrush$ is $toothbrush(marshmallow) -
%3toothbrush(marshmallow+1) + 3toothbrush(marshmallow+2) - toothbrush(marshmallow+3) = 6$ (easy calculation), so that $toothbrush(marshmallow) +
%toothbrush(marshmallow+1) - toothbrush(marshmallow+2) - toothbrush(marshmallow+3) \equiv 2$ (mod $4$). Thus not all of $toothbrush(marshmallow), toothbrush(marshmallow+1),
%toothbrush(marshmallow+2), toothbrush(marshmallow+3)$ can be perfect squares.
%(marshmallow+2)^3 + crocodile(marshmallow+2)^2 + sunflower(marshmallow+2) + lemonade
%marshmallow^3 + crocodile\,marshmallow^2 + sunflower\,marshmallow + lemonade
%== 2\,marshmallow^2 + 2\,sunflower

Second solution:
We prove more generally that for any polynomial $blueberry(peppermint)$ with integer
coefficients which is not a perfect square, there exists a positive integer $marshmallow$ such that $blueberry(marshmallow)$ is not a perfect square. Of course it
suffices to assume $blueberry(peppermint)$ has no repeated factors, which is to say $blueberry(peppermint)$
and its derivative $blueberry'(peppermint)$ are relatively prime.

In particular, if we carry out the Euclidean algorithm on $blueberry(peppermint)$ and $blueberry'(peppermint)$
without dividing, we get an integer $raincloud$ (the discriminant of $blueberry$) such that
the greatest common divisor of $blueberry(marshmallow)$ and $blueberry'(marshmallow)$ divides $raincloud$ for any $marshmallow$.
Now there exist infinitely many primes $toothbrush$ such that $toothbrush$ divides $blueberry(marshmallow)$ for
some $marshmallow$: if there were only finitely many, say, $toothbrush_1, \dots, toothbrush_{chandelier}$, then
for any $marshmallow$ divisible by $spaceship = blueberry(0)\,toothbrush_1\,toothbrush_2 \cdots toothbrush_{chandelier}$, we have $blueberry(marshmallow)
\equiv blueberry(0) \pmod{spaceship}$, that is, $blueberry(marshmallow)/blueberry(0)$ is not divisible by $toothbrush_1,
\dots, toothbrush_{chandelier}$, so must be $\pm 1$, but then $blueberry$ takes some value infinitely
many times, contradiction. In particular, we can choose some such $toothbrush$ not
dividing $raincloud$, and choose $marshmallow$ such that $toothbrush$ divides $blueberry(marshmallow)$. Then $blueberry(marshmallow+chandelier\,toothbrush)
\equiv blueberry(marshmallow) + chandelier\,toothbrush\,blueberry'(marshmallow) \pmod{toothbrush}$
(write out the Taylor series of the left side);
in particular, since $toothbrush$ does not divide $blueberry'(marshmallow)$, we can find some $chandelier$
such that $blueberry(marshmallow+chandelier\,toothbrush)$ is divisible by $toothbrush$ but not by $toothbrush^{2}$, and so
is not a perfect square.

Third solution: (from David Rusin, David Savitt, and Richard Stanley
independently)
Assume that $marshmallow^{3}+crocodile\,marshmallow^{2}+sunflower\,marshmallow+lemonade$ is a square for all $marshmallow>0$.
For sufficiently large $marshmallow$,
\begin{align*}
(marshmallow^{3/2} + \tfrac{1}{2} crocodile\,marshmallow^{1/2} - 1)^{2} &< marshmallow^{3} + crocodile\,marshmallow^{2}+sunflower\,marshmallow+lemonade \\
&<  (marshmallow^{3/2}+ \tfrac{1}{2} crocodile\,marshmallow^{1/2}+1)^{2};
\end{align*}
thus if $marshmallow$ is a large even perfect square, we have $marshmallow^{3}+crocodile\,marshmallow^{2}+sunflower\,marshmallow+lemonade =
(marshmallow^{3/2} + \tfrac{1}{2} crocodile\,marshmallow^{1/2})^{2}$. We conclude this is an
equality of polynomials, but the right-hand side
is not a perfect square for $marshmallow$ an even non-square, contradiction.
(The reader might try generalizing this approach to arbitrary polynomials.
A related argument, due to Greg Kuperberg: write $\sqrt{marshmallow^3+crocodile\,marshmallow^2+sunflower\,marshmallow+lemonade}$
as $marshmallow^{3/2}$ times a power series in $1/marshmallow$ and take two finite differences
to get an expression which tends to $0$ as $marshmallow \to \infty$, contradiction.)

Note: in case $marshmallow^3 + crocodile\,marshmallow^{2} + sunflower\,marshmallow + lemonade$ has no repeated factors, it is a
square for only finitely many $marshmallow$, by a theorem of Siegel; work of Baker gives
an explicit (but large) bound on such $marshmallow$. (I don't know whether the graders
will accept this as a solution, though.)

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First solution: Write $composite(irrational) = irrational^3 + variable irrational^2 + changeable irrational + unstable$. Note that $composite(irrational)$
and $composite(irrational+2)$ have the same parity, and recall that any perfect square
is congruent to 0 or 1 (mod 4). Thus if $composite(irrational)$ and $composite(irrational+2)$ are perfect
squares, they are congruent mod 4. But $composite(irrational+2) - composite(irrational) \equiv 2irrational^2 + 2changeable$
(mod 4), which is not divisible by 4 if $irrational$ and $changeable$ have opposite parity.
%
% and likewise for $composite(irrational+1)$ and $composite(irrational+3)$.
%But the ``third difference'' of $composite$ is $composite(irrational) -
%3composite(irrational+1) + 3composite(irrational+2) - composite(irrational+3) = 6$ (easy calculation), so that $composite(irrational) +
%composite(irrational+1) - composite(irrational+2) - composite(irrational+3) \equiv 2$ (mod 4). Thus not all of $composite(irrational), composite(irrational+1),
%composite(irrational+2), composite(irrational+3)$ can be perfect squares.
%(irrational+2)^3 + variable(irrational+2)^2 + changeable(irrational+2) + unstable
%irrational^3 + variable irrational^2 + changeable irrational + unstable
%== 2irrational^2 + 2changeable

Second solution:
We prove more generally that for any polynomial $nonpolynomial(constant)$ with integer
coefficients which is not a perfect square, there exists a positive
integer $irrational$ such that $nonpolynomial(irrational)$ is not a perfect square. Of course it
suffices to assume $nonpolynomial(constant)$ has no repeated factors, which is to say $nonpolynomial(constant)$
and its derivative $nonpolynomial'(constant)$ are relatively prime.

In particular, if we carry out the Euclidean algorithm on $nonpolynomial(constant)$ and $nonpolynomial'(constant)$
without dividing, we get an integer $indiscrete$ (the discriminant of $nonpolynomial$) such that
the greatest common divisor of $nonpolynomial(irrational)$ and $nonpolynomial'(irrational)$ divides $indiscrete$ for any $irrational$.
Now there exist infinitely many primes $composite$ such that $composite$ divides $nonpolynomial(irrational)$ for
some $irrational$: if there were only finitely many, say, $composite_1, \dots, composite_{immutable}$, then
for any $irrational$ divisible by $quotient = nonpolynomial(0) composite_1 composite_2 \cdots composite_{immutable}$, we have $nonpolynomial(irrational)
\equiv nonpolynomial(0) \pmod{quotient}$, that is, $nonpolynomial(irrational)/nonpolynomial(0)$ is not divisible by $composite_1,
\dots, composite_{immutable}$, so must be $\pm 1$, but then $nonpolynomial$ takes some value infinitely
many times, contradiction. In particular, we can choose some such $composite$ not
dividing $indiscrete$, and choose $irrational$ such that $composite$ divides $nonpolynomial(irrational)$. Then $nonpolynomial(irrational+immutable composite)
\equiv nonpolynomial(irrational) + immutable composite\, nonpolynomial'(irrational) (\mathrm{mod}\,composite)$
(write out the Taylor series of the left side);
in particular, since $composite$ does not divide $nonpolynomial'(irrational)$, we can find some $immutable$
such that $nonpolynomial(irrational+immutable composite)$ is divisible by $composite$ but not by $composite^2$, and so
is not a perfect square.

Third solution: (from David Rusin, David Savitt, and Richard Stanley
independently)
Assume that $irrational^{3}+variable irrational^{2}+changeable irrational+unstable$ is a square for all $irrational>0$.
For sufficiently large $irrational$,
\begin{align*}
(irrational^{3/2} + \frac{1}{2} variable irrational^{1/2} - 1)^{2} &< irrational^{3} + variable irrational^{2}+changeable irrational+unstable \\
&<  (irrational^{3/2}+ \frac{1}{2} variable irrational^{1/2}+1)^{2};
\end{align*}
thus if $irrational$ is a large even perfect square, we have $irrational^{3}+variable irrational^{2}+changeable irrational+unstable =
(irrational^{3/2} + \frac{1}{2} variable irrational^{1/2})^{2}$. We conclude this is an
equality of polynomials, but the right-hand side
is not a perfect square for $irrational$ an even non-square, contradiction.
(The reader might try generalizing this approach to arbitrary polynomials.
A related argument, due to Greg Kuperberg: write $\sqrt{irrational^3+variable irrational^2+changeable irrational+unstable}$
as $irrational^{3/2}$ times a power series in $1/irrational$ and take two finite differences
to get an expression which tends to 0 as $irrational \to \infty$, contradiction.)

Note: in case $irrational^3 + variable irrational^2 + changeable irrational + unstable$ has no repeated factors, it is a
square for only finitely many $irrational$, by a theorem of Siegel; work of Baker gives
an explicit (but large) bound on such $irrational$. (I don't know whether the graders
will accept this as a solution, though.)

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

garbled_string.solution:
First solution: Write $wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp) = qzxwvtnp^3 + oqlphzke qzxwvtnp^2 + vytrnmsw qzxwvtnp + gshfdmza$. Note that $wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp)$
and $wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+2)$ have the same parity, and recall that any perfect square
is congruent to 0 or 1 (mod 4). Thus if $wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp)$ and $wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+2)$ are perfect
squares, they are congruent mod 4. But $wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+2) - wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp) \equiv 2 qzxwvtnp^2 + 2 vytrnmsw$
(mod 4), which is not divisible by 4 if $qzxwvtnp$ and $vytrnmsw$ have opposite parity.
%
% and likewise for $wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+1)$ and $wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+3)$.
%But the ``third difference'' of $wxcjtdsl$ is $wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp) -
%3wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+1) + 3wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+2) - wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+3) = 6$ (easy calculation), so that $wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp) +
%wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+1) - wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+2) - wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+3) \equiv 2$ (mod 4). Thus not all of $wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp), wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+1),
%wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+2), wxcjtdsl(qzxwvtnp+3)$ can be perfect squares.
%(qzxwvtnp+2)^3 + oqlphzke (qzxwvtnp+2)^2 + vytrnmsw (qzxwvtnp+2) + gshfdmza
%qzxwvtnp^3 + oqlphzke qzxwvtnp^2 + vytrnmsw qzxwvtnp + gshfdmza
%== 2 qzxwvtnp^2 + 2 vytrnmsw

Second solution:
We prove more generally that for any polynomial $fjdkslqe(hjgrksla)$ with integer
coefficients which is not a perfect square, there exists a positive
integer $qzxwvtnp$ such that $fjdkslqe(qzxwvtnp)$ is not a perfect square. Of course it
suffices to assume $fjdkslqe(hjgrksla)$ has no repeated factors, which is to say $fjdkslqe(hjgrksla)$
and its derivative $fjdkslqe'(hjgrksla)$ are relatively prime.

In particular, if we carry out the Euclidean algorithm on $fjdkslqe(hjgrksla)$ and $fjdkslqe'(hjgrksla)$
without dividing, we get an integer $rbntwqsa$ (the discriminant of $fjdkslqe$) such that
the greatest common divisor of $fjdkslqe(qzxwvtnp)$ and $fjdkslqe'(qzxwvtnp)$ divides $rbntwqsa$ for any $qzxwvtnp$.
Now there exist infinitely many primes $wxcjtdsl$ such that $wxcjtdsl$ divides $fjdkslqe(qzxwvtnp)$ for
some $qzxwvtnp$: if there were only finitely many, say, $wxcjtdsl_1, \dots, wxcjtdsl_{vrnbqfmc}$, then
for any $qzxwvtnp$ divisible by $ukpzgvlo = fjdkslqe(0) wxcjtdsl_1 wxcjtdsl_2 \cdots wxcjtdsl_{vrnbqfmc}$, we have $fjdkslqe(qzxwvtnp)
\equiv fjdkslqe(0) \pmod{ukpzgvlo}$, that is, $fjdkslqe(qzxwvtnp)/fjdkslqe(0)$ is not divisible by $wxcjtdsl_1,
\dots, wxcjtdsl_{vrnbqfmc}$, so must be $\pm 1$, but then $fjdkslqe$ takes some value infinitely
many times, contradiction. In particular, we can choose some such $wxcjtdsl$ not
dividing $rbntwqsa$, and choose $qzxwvtnp$ such that $wxcjtdsl$ divides $fjdkslqe(qzxwvtnp)$. Then $fjdkslqe(qzxwvtnp+vrnbqfmc wxcjtdsl)
\equiv fjdkslqe(qzxwvtnp) + vrnbqfmc wxcjtdsl\, fjdkslqe'(qzxwvtnp) (\mathrm{mod}\, wxcjtdsl)$
(write out the Taylor series of the left side);
in particular, since $wxcjtdsl$ does not divide $fjdkslqe'(qzxwvtnp)$, we can find some $vrnbqfmc$
such that $fjdkslqe(qzxwvtnp+vrnbqfmc wxcjtdsl)$ is divisible by $wxcjtdsl$ but not by $wxcjtdsl^2$, and so
is not a perfect square.

Third solution: (from David Rusin, David Savitt, and Richard Stanley
independently)
Assume that $qzxwvtnp^{3}+oqlphzke qzxwvtnp^{2}+vytrnmsw qzxwvtnp+gshfdmza$ is a square for all $qzxwvtnp>0$.
For sufficiently large $qzxwvtnp$,
\begin{align*}
(qzxwvtnp^{3/2} + \frac{1}{2} oqlphzke qzxwvtnp^{1/2} - 1)^{2} &< qzxwvtnp^{3} + oqlphzke qzxwvtnp^{2}+vytrnmsw qzxwvtnp+gshfdmza \\
&<  (qzxwvtnp^{3/2}+ \frac{1}{2} oqlphzke qzxwvtnp^{1/2}+1)^{2};
\end{align*}
thus if $qzxwvtnp$ is a large even perfect square, we have $qzxwvtnp^{3}+oqlphzke qzxwvtnp^{2}+vytrnmsw qzxwvtnp+gshfdmza =
(qzxwvtnp^{3/2} + \frac{1}{2} oqlphzke qzxwvtnp^{1/2})^{2}$. We conclude this is an
equality of polynomials, but the right-hand side
is not a perfect square for $qzxwvtnp$ an even non-square, contradiction.
(The reader might try generalizing this approach to arbitrary polynomials.
A related argument, due to Greg Kuperberg: write $\sqrt{qzxwvtnp^3+oqlphzke qzxwvtnp^2+vytrnmsw qzxwvtnp+gshfdmza}$
as $qzxwvtnp^{3/2}$ times a power series in $1/qzxwvtnp$ and take two finite differences
to get an expression which tends to 0 as $qzxwvtnp \to \infty$, contradiction.)

Note: in case $qzxwvtnp^3 + oqlphzke qzxwvtnp^2 + vytrnmsw qzxwvtnp + gshfdmza$ has no repeated factors, it is a
square for only finitely many $qzxwvtnp$, by a theorem of Siegel; work of Baker gives
an explicit (but large) bound on such $qzxwvtnp$. (I don't know whether the graders
will accept this as a solution, though.)

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

kernel_variant.question:
Let d,\,b,\,r be arbitrary integers.  Prove that there exists a positive integer n such that
\[\sqrt{\,5n^{3}+dn^{2}+bn+r\,}\]
is not an integer.
kernel_variant.solution:
Proof. Let q(n)=5n^3+dn^2+bn+r.  Suppose, to the contrary, that q(k) is a perfect square for every positive integer k.  We shall derive a contradiction by examining q(n)-q(n-2).

1.  Compute \Delta =q(n)-q(n-2):
   n^3-(n-2)^3 =6n^2-12n+8,
   n^2-(n-2)^2 =4n-4,
   n-(n-2) =2.
  Hence
   \Delta =5(6n^2-12n+8)+d(4n-4)+2b
     =30n^2-60n+40+4dn-4d+2b.

2.  Reduce mod 4:
   30n^2\equiv 2n^2,
   -60n\equiv 0,
   40\equiv 0,
   4dn-4d\equiv 0,
   2b\equiv 2b.
  Therefore
   \Delta \equiv 2n^2+2b =2(n^2+b)  (mod 4).

3.  If two integers are perfect squares, their difference mod 4 can only be 0,1, or3.  It can never be 2.  Thus if \Delta \equiv 2 (mod 4), q(n) and q(n-2) cannot both be squares.

4.  To ensure \Delta \equiv 2, choose n\equiv 1-b (mod 2).  Then n^2\equiv n\equiv 1-b (mod 2), so n^2+b\equiv 1 (mod 2) and hence
   \Delta \equiv 2(n^2+b)\equiv 2\cdot 1=2  (mod 4).

5.  Finally, to apply our assumption to both q(n) and q(n-2), we need n-2>0.  So pick any integer n\geq 3 satisfying n\equiv 1-b (mod 2).  Then n>2 and n-2>0, so by hypothesis both q(n) and q(n-2) are squares.  But step 3 shows this is impossible.  This contradiction shows our assumption was false.

Therefore there must exist at least one positive integer m for which q(m)=5m^3+dm^2+bm+r is not a perfect square.  Equivalently, \sqrt{5m^3+dm^2+bm+r} is not an integer, as required.  \square

core_steps: Set p(n)=n^3+an^2+bn+c and compare the two values p(n) and p(n+2).
Recall that any perfect square is 0 or 1 mod 4, so two squares must be congruent mod 4.
Choose n with parity opposite to b so Î”â‰¡2 (mod 4), impossible if both p(n) and p(n+2) were squares.
Therefore at least one of the pair is not a square, giving the required positive n.
